g.data-narration: Welcome to Public Key Cryptanalysis. We'll explore how attackers break asymmetric cryptosystems and learn essential defenses.
Cryptanalysis is the science of breaking cryptographic systems without direct access to the secret key. Attackers exploit mathematical weaknesses and implementation flaws.
g.text: The science of breaking public key cryptosystems without having access to the secret key.
Unlike symmetric crypto, attackers have the public key - additional information to exploit.

g.data-narration: Public key systems give attackers more information than symmetric systems. The public key itself can be a target for mathematical attacks like factorization.
g.text: Attacker's Advantage in Public Key Systems

g.data-narration: Brute force tries all possible keys. While simple, modern key sizes make this impractical. RSA-512 was broken in 1999, but RSA-2048 remains secure.
g.text: Systematically trying all possible private keys until the correct one is found.

g.data-narration: Integer factorization attacks RSA by computing the private key from the public modulus n=p×q. The General Number Field Sieve is the most efficient method.
g.text: Target: Factor n = p × q to discover the private key d from public key (n, e)
n = p × q → Find p, q → Compute d = e⁻¹ mod (p-1)(q-1)

g.data-narration: The GNFS factored RSA-768 in 2009. RSA-829 (250 digits) was broken in 2020. RSA-1024 is considered at risk, but RSA-2048 remains secure.
Discrete logarithm attacks target Diffie-Hellman, DSA, and ECC. Given g^x mod p, finding x breaks key exchange and signature schemes.
g.text: Targets: Diffie-Hellman, ElGamal, DSA, Elliptic Curve Cryptography
Given: g^x mod p → Find: x

g.data-narration: When e=3 and the same message goes to three recipients, Chinese Remainder Theorem recovers plaintext without factoring any modulus.
g.text: If the same message M is encrypted to 3+ recipients with e=3, CRT recovers M

g.data-narration: Timing attacks measure computation time to deduce private key bits. If bit=1, multiplication occurs; if bit=0, only squaring. These micro-differences reveal the key.
g.text: RSA decryption time varies based on private key bits. Measuring thousands of operations reveals the key.

g.data-narration: CCA attacks trick RSA decryption into revealing plaintext. Attackers manipulate ciphertexts and use the decryption oracle to extract secrets.
g.text: Attacker chooses ciphertexts and obtains decryptions to break the system

g.data-narration: Man-in-the-Middle attackers intercept key exchange, impersonating both parties. They establish separate keys with each victim while appearing as the trusted intermediary.
g.text: Attacker intercepts communication and impersonates both parties during key exchange

g.data-narration: When two users share the same RSA modulus n but different exponents, messages encrypted for one can be decrypted by knowing either key pair.
g.text: Two users share modulus n but have different (e, d) pairs
Anyone can decrypt without knowing d

g.data-narration: Wiener's attack exploits small private exponents. When d is less than n^0.25, continued fractions can recover d efficiently, breaking RSA.
g.text: If private exponent d is chosen too small for efficiency, it can be recovered

g.data-narration: Different attacks target different vulnerabilities. Mathematical attacks improve over time, while implementation attacks require secure coding practices.
Shor's Algorithm can factor integers in polynomial time using quantum computers. This would break RSA, DH, DSA, and ECC. Post-quantum cryptography is under development.
g.text: A quantum algorithm that can factor integers and solve discrete logs in polynomial time
Defense: NIST Post-Quantum Standardization (Lattice-based, Code-based, Hash-based)

g.data-narration: Defense in depth: use large keys (2048+ bits), proper padding (OAEP/PSS), constant-time code, and certificate validation. Plan for post-quantum migration.
